text: Investigating the transport mechanisms of mercury ions (Hg(II)) in the human intestine has traditionally relied on animal models or static cell cultures, both of which fail to accurately replicate the complex physiological microenvironment or provide real-time monitoring of cellular responses. In this study, we developed a biomimetic gut-on-a-chip platform integrated with label-free sensors to simulate intestinal physiology and monitor Hg(II) absorption in situ. The device recreates the dynamic intestinal environment by applying precise fluid shear stress (0.02 dyne/cm²) and cyclic mechanical strain (1%, 0.15 Hz). Integrated transepithelial electrical resistance (TEER) sensors allowed for the noninvasive tracking of tight junction formation and barrier integrity, while electrochemical sensors enabled the quantification of Hg(II) transport. Our results demonstrate that the combination of fluid flow and mechanical stretching promotes the formation of intestinal villi-like structures and enhances cellular differentiation compared to static models. Exposure experiments revealed that Hg(II) causes significant physical damage to the epithelial barrier, evidenced by a decrease in TEER and downregulation of ZO-1 and ezrin proteins. Furthermore, we observed that increasing mechanical tensile strain from 1% to 5% led to a 23.59% increase in Hg(II) absorption, a phenomenon correlated with the upregulated expression of the mechanosensitive channel Piezo1 and the metal transporter DMT1. This integrated organ-on-a-chip system provides a robust tool for studying heavy metal toxicology and understanding the mechanical regulation of intestinal absorption.
